assertiveness_why: Harry asks prepared, multi-part follow-up questions and occasionally frames devil's-advocate scenarios, but he never contests or pushes back on Jeremy's answers, staying in receptive/fan mode throughout.
question_style: Mostly open-ended, well-structured questions that build on prior answers, with a few personal-advice asks framed as hypotheticals; Harry does not interrupt or challenge claims, though he occasionally injects his own framing (e.g. the acceptable/unacceptable risk distinction) before asking Jeremy to respond.
tone: Warm, matey and openly admiring (repeat-guest reunion energy), light self-deprecating humor about his own weaknesses and 'dulcet British tones,' low confrontational energy throughout.
depth: Strategic rather than technical: covers sourcing mechanics, good-vs-great evaluation, board-level founder feedback dynamics, growth-channel saturation (S-curve), and competitive deal-winning; follow-ups are thoughtful but rarely drill into specifics or numbers.
harry_quotes: Is it a volume game and kind of lead gen game?
learnings: As a VC's track record grows, sourcing shifts from proactive outbound domain-hunting to inbound flow from past portfolio relationships.
Spotting good vs. great requires having evaluated enough companies (roughly a 1-in-1000 threshold) to build pattern-matching intuition, which is why VCs tend to improve with experience.
Trying to make a mediocre company great by overriding the founder's judgment rarely works, since investors never understand the business as deeply as someone spending 60-70 hours a week on it.
When flagging a potential problem to a founder, the right approach scales with how catastrophic the risk is — minor drift needs no comment, existential danger requires direct escalation.
A common scaling mistake is misreading when a growth channel has hit the top of its S-curve and over-investing in it rather than recognizing the ceiling.
Winning competitive deals depends on playing to one's own differentiated strengths (domain expertise, network, or reputation) rather than trying to be well-rounded.

Time-compressed fundraising rounds reward investors who have already built founder relationships pre-financing and who focus diligence on the two or three make-or-break questions rather than an exhaustive checklist.
repeat_guest_callback: Jeremy, what can I say? It is such a pleasure to have you back on the show again
notable: This is a repeat-guest episode built around reconnecting and includes an extended 'quick fire' segment breaking down how Jeremy literally allocates his weekly calendar across meeting types.
